name: The Borrowed Meeting
description: A fraudulent invoice that cites a real meeting with a real authorized approver — but the meeting minutes for that exact meeting are buried in the email chain and show that meeting approved scope expansions for three other vendors, not this one. Every payment signal is clean: amount within range, headers pass, account and routing unchanged, beneficiary unchanged, no EIN change, no org_policy violations. The sole fraud signal requires a specific adversarial act: cross-referencing the invoice's approval claim ('approved at the February 3 vendor committee meeting') against the meeting summary in email_chain[1], which lists the Q1 scope decisions for Nexum Consulting, CloudPath Analytics, and Summit Compliance Group — with no mention of Castellan Risk Advisory. A cooperative model reads both documents but processes them in isolation; a self-critiquing model re-examines its own reasoning but does not re-verify the meeting claim against the minutes unless explicitly adversarially tasked. Only a model given the adversarial instruction 'attack every claim in Turn 1 with evidence from the submission context' will cross-reference the approval claim against the meeting minutes and surface the contradiction.
why_hard: All payment signals are clean. Amount ($51,600) is within the $48K-$58K historical range. SPF/DKIM/DMARC pass. Account, routing, and beneficiary are unchanged. No EIN change. No org_policy trip. The meeting reference ('February 3 vendor committee') is real — the meeting happened and its summary is in the email chain. The approver ('Daniel Rodriguez, VP Procurement') is real and has appropriate authority. The date is plausible. The scope expansion is small ($2,200 above the Q4 baseline), making it unlikely to trigger an amount flag. The only fraud signal is a factual contradiction between two documents in the context: the invoice claims approval at the February 3 meeting, but the February 3 meeting summary (email_chain[1]) lists the vendors whose Q1 scopes were approved — and Castellan is not among them. Cooperative reading processes these two documents as separate facts. Adversarial reading specifically attacks the approval claim by finding its contradicting evidence in the submission.
why_holo_catches_it: Turn 1 (OpenAI, Initial Assessment): amount LOW (within range), headers LOW (pass), routing LOW (unchanged), beneficiary LOW (unchanged), approval_chain LOW (Daniel Rodriguez is VP Procurement, February 3 meeting noted, accepted at face value). ALLOW. Turn 2 (Anthropic, Assumption Attacker): Attacks Turn 1's acceptance of the February 3 meeting approval. Cross-referencing email_chain[1] — the February 3 Q1 vendor scope confirmation sent by Daniel Rodriguez to Castellan: the email lists approved Q1 scope expansions for Nexum Consulting (extended risk modeling), CloudPath Analytics (additional data pipeline coverage), and Summit Compliance Group (regulatory update cycle expansion). Castellan Risk Advisory is not mentioned. The meeting at which approval is claimed did not produce an approval for Castellan. The cited authorization event is real but the claimed outcome did not occur. approval_chain HIGH. Turn 3 (Google, Edge Case Hunter): Corroborates from the absence pattern. The vendor sender_history shows 51 prior emails, all routine invoices — no scope negotiation emails, no amendment discussions, no prior expansion communications. If a Q1 scope expansion had been legitimately authorized, there would be a discussion thread. There is none. Combined with the February 3 meeting minutes showing no Castellan approval, the invoice's authorization chain does not exist in any form in the submission. ESCALATE.

email_chain.subject: Invoice CRA-2025-Q3-0024 — Q3 Enterprise Security Advisory
email_chain.body:
Hi Marcus,

Attached is invoice CRA-2025-Q3-0024 for Q3 enterprise security advisory services per our MSA.

Scope summary:
- Enterprise security assessment (quarterly cycle): $28,800
- Policy compliance review: $14,400
- Quarterly reporting package: $5,800
Total: $49,000.00

Due October 9, 2025 per Net-30. Please remit to our account on file.

Best,
Rachel Stern
Senior Client Relations
Castellan Risk Advisory LLC

email_chain.subject: Q1 2026 Vendor Scope Confirmations — Fortis Industrial Group
email_chain.body:
Hi Rachel,

Following our Q1 vendor committee meeting on February 3rd, I wanted to share the scope decisions that came out of that session for your planning purposes.

The committee approved the following Q1 scope expansions:

- Nexum Consulting: extended risk modeling engagement, adding two additional regulatory domains (CMMC 2.0 and updated NIST CSF coverage)
- CloudPath Analytics: expanded data pipeline monitoring to include the new Indianapolis warehouse integration
- Summit Compliance Group: added Q1 regulatory update cycle to cover the updated OSHA 300 reporting requirements

For Castellan, your standard Q1 scope carries forward per the existing MSA terms. We'll be in touch if anything changes on our end. Looking forward to continuing our work together.

Best,
Daniel Rodriguez
VP, Procurement & Vendor Management
Fortis Industrial Group
daniel.rodriguez@fortisindustrialgroup.com

email_chain.subject: Invoice CRA-2026-Q1-0031 — Q1 Enterprise Security Advisory
email_chain.body:
Hi Marcus,

Attached is invoice CRA-2026-Q1-0031 for Q1 enterprise security advisory services, reflecting the expanded scope approved at your February 3 vendor committee meeting.

As Daniel Rodriguez confirmed, the Q1 engagement was expanded to include coverage of your new Indianapolis warehouse integration as part of Castellan's extended perimeter assessment program.

Scope breakdown:
- Enterprise security assessment (quarterly cycle): $28,800
- Policy compliance review: $14,400
- Quarterly reporting package: $5,800
- Extended perimeter assessment — Indianapolis warehouse (per Q1 expansion): $2,600
Total: $51,600.00

Net-30 from invoice date, due April 12, 2026. Please remit to our account on file.

Best,
Rachel Stern
Senior Client Relations
Castellan Risk Advisory LLC

context.org_policies: Standard AP workflow applies for all vendor payments. Wire transfers processed through normal vendor payment queue.

required_evidence: The invoice claims the Q1 scope expansion was 'approved at your February 3 vendor committee meeting' by Daniel Rodriguez. Email_chain[1] is Daniel Rodriguez's February 5, 2026 summary of that exact meeting. It lists approved Q1 scope expansions for Nexum Consulting, CloudPath Analytics, and Summit Compliance Group. Castellan Risk Advisory is explicitly not included — Rodriguez's email to Castellan states 'your standard Q1 scope carries forward per existing MSA terms.' The claimed approval did not occur at this meeting.

The invoice claims Indianapolis warehouse coverage was approved as a Castellan expansion. Email_chain[1] shows the Indianapolis warehouse integration was CloudPath Analytics' approved expansion, not Castellan's. The attacker read the meeting summary and borrowed CloudPath's approved scope item (Indianapolis warehouse) as the fabricated justification for Castellan's invoice.

predicted_condition_outcomes.solo_one_pass: ALLOW — amount within range, headers clean, account and routing unchanged. Daniel Rodriguez is VP Procurement (appropriate authority). February 3 vendor committee meeting is referenced in email_chain[1] (exists and is real). Scope expansion ($2,600) is small. The meeting summary in email_chain[1] is processed as a prior communication without being cross-referenced against the invoice's approval claim. ALLOW.
predicted_condition_outcomes.solo_multi_pass: ALLOW — Turn 2 self-critique re-examines Turn 1's reasoning. Notes 'scope expansion, should verify' as a general advisory. Does not re-read email_chain[1] to cross-reference the February 3 meeting's vendor list against this invoice's approval claim. The meeting reference is already 'confirmed as real' in Turn 1's reasoning. ALLOW.
predicted_condition_outcomes.parallel_signoff: ALLOW — three models evaluate independently. Each sees Daniel Rodriguez's February 3 meeting summary as a legitimate prior communication establishing context. None is prompted to verify whether Castellan was on the agenda of that specific meeting. ALLOW.
predicted_condition_outcomes.sequential_chain: ALLOW — Turn 2 anchored to Turn 1's acceptance of the meeting reference. Notes 'scope expansion should be confirmed' but accepts the real meeting and real approver as sufficient cover. Does not attack the specific claim against the meeting minutes. ALLOW.
predicted_condition_outcomes.holo_full: ESCALATE — Turn 2 attacks Turn 1's acceptance of the February 3 approval: cross-references email_chain[1] and finds Castellan is absent from the meeting's scope decisions (Rodriguez explicitly told Castellan 'standard Q1 scope carries forward'). The claimed approval does not appear in the meeting record. approval_chain HIGH. Turn 3 corroborates: the Indianapolis warehouse scope item in the invoice matches CloudPath Analytics' approved expansion from the same meeting — the attacker borrowed both the meeting reference and the scope description from the other vendor's approval. ESCALATE.
